contents:
(** * BAut(Cantor) *)

(** ** A pre-idempotent on [BAut Cantor] that does not split *)

(** We go into a non-exported module so that we can use short names for definitions without polluting the global namespace. *)
Definition f : BAut Cantor -> BAut Cantor.
(** Here is the important part of this definition. *)
(** The rest is just a proof that [Z+Cantor] is again equivalent to [Cantor], using [cantor_fold] and the assumption that [Z] is equivalent to [Cantor]. *)
pose (e := Z.2); simpl in e; clearbody e.
(** For the pre-idempotence of [f], the main point is again the existence of the equivalence [fold_cantor]. *)
refine (_ oE equiv_sum_assoc Z Cantor Cantor).
(** We record how the action of [f] and [f o f] on paths corresponds to an action on equivalences. *)

Definition ap_f {Z Z' : BAut Cantor} (p : Z = Z')
  : equiv_path _ _ (ap f p)..1
    = equiv_path Z Z' p..1 +E 1.

goals.conclusion: equiv_path (f Z).1 (f Z').1 (ap f p) ..1 = equiv_path Z Z' p ..1 +E 1
equiv_path (f Z).1 (f Z').1 (ap f p) ..1 = equiv_path Z Z' p ..1 +E 1
equiv_path (f Z).1 (f Z).1 (ap f 1) ..1 = equiv_path Z Z 1 ..1 +E 1
equiv_path (f Z).1 (f Z).1 (ap f 1) ..1 == equiv_path Z Z 1 ..1 +E 1

contents:
Definition ap_ff {Z Z' : BAut Cantor} (p : Z = Z')
  : equiv_path _ _ (ap (f o f) p)..1
    = equiv_path Z Z' p..1 +E 1 +E 1.

goals.conclusion:
equiv_path (f (f Z)).1 (f (f Z')).1 (ap (f o f) p) ..1 =
equiv_path Z Z' p ..1 +E 1 +E 1

equiv_path (f (f Z)).1 (f (f Z')).1 (ap (f o f) p) ..1 =
equiv_path Z Z' p ..1 +E 1 +E 1

equiv_path (f (f Z)).1 (f (f Z)).1
  (ap (fun x : BAut Cantor => f (f x)) 1) ..1 =
equiv_path Z Z 1 ..1 +E 1 +E 1

equiv_path (f (f Z)).1 (f (f Z)).1
  (ap (fun x : BAut Cantor => f (f x)) 1) ..1 ==
equiv_path Z Z 1 ..1 +E 1 +E 1

contents: (** Now let's assume [f] is quasi-idempotent, but not necessarily using the same witness of pre-idempotency. *)

Definition I (Z : BAut Cantor)
  : (Z + Cantor) + Cantor <~> Z + Cantor
    := equiv_path _ _ (Ip Z)..1.

Definition I0
  : Cantor + Cantor + Cantor + Cantor <~> Cantor + Cantor + Cantor
    := I (f (point (BAut Cantor))).
(** We don't know much about [I0], but we can show that it maps the rightmost two summands to the rightmost one, using the naturality of [I].  Here is the naturality. *)

Definition Inat (Z Z' : BAut Cantor) (e : Z <~> Z')
  : I Z' oE (e +E 1 +E 1)
    = (e +E 1) oE I Z.

goals.conclusion: I Z' oE (e +E 1 +E 1) = (e +E 1) oE I Z
I Z' oE (e +E 1 +E 1) = (e +E 1) oE I Z

contents: revert e; equiv_intro (equiv_path Z Z') q.

goals.conclusion: I Z' oE (equiv_path Z Z' q +E 1 +E 1) = (equiv_path Z Z' q +E 1) oE I Z

contents: revert q; equiv_intro ((equiv_path_sigma_hprop Z Z')^-1) p.

goals.conclusion:
I Z' oE (equiv_path Z Z' ((equiv_path_sigma_hprop Z Z')^-1 p) +E 1 +E 1) =
(equiv_path Z Z' ((equiv_path_sigma_hprop Z Z')^-1 p) +E 1) oE I Z

I Z' oE (equiv_path Z Z' p ..1 +E 1 +E 1) =
(equiv_path Z Z' p ..1 +E 1) oE I Z

I Z'
oE equiv_path (f (f Z)).1 (f (f Z')).1
     (ap (fun x : BAut Cantor => f (f x)) p) ..1 =
equiv_path (f Z).1 (f Z').1 (ap f p) ..1 oE I Z

equiv_path (f (f Z')).1 (f Z').1 (Ip Z') ..1
oE equiv_path (f (f Z)).1 (f (f Z')).1
     (ap (fun x : BAut Cantor => f (f x)) p) ..1 =
equiv_path (f Z).1 (f Z').1 (ap f p) ..1
oE equiv_path (f (f Z)).1 (f Z).1 (Ip Z) ..1

equiv_path (Z + Cantor + Cantor) (Z' + Cantor)
  ((ap (fun x : BAut Cantor => f (f x)) p) ..1 @ (Ip Z') ..1) =
equiv_path (Z + Cantor + Cantor) (Z' + Cantor) ((Ip Z) ..1 @ (ap f p) ..1)

(ap (fun x : BAut Cantor => f (f x)) p) ..1 @ (Ip Z') ..1 =
(Ip Z) ..1 @ (ap f p) ..1

contents: refine ((pr1_path_pp (ap (f o f) p) (Ip Z'))^ @ _ @ pr1_path_pp _ _).

goals.conclusion: (ap (fun x : BAut Cantor => f (f x)) p @ Ip Z') ..1 = (Ip Z @ ap f p) ..1
ap (fun x : BAut Cantor => f (f x)) p @ Ip Z' = Ip Z @ ap f p

contents: (** To show our claim about the action of [I0], we will apply this naturality to the flip automorphism of [Cantor + Cantor].  Here are the images of that automorphism under [f] and [f o f]. *)

Definition f_flip :=
    equiv_sum_symm Cantor Cantor +E equiv_idmap Cantor.

Definition ff_flip :=
    (equiv_sum_symm Cantor Cantor +E equiv_idmap Cantor) +E (equiv_idmap Cantor).
(** The naturality of [I] implies that [I0] commutes with these images of the flip. *)

Definition I0nat_flip
        (x : ((Cantor + Cantor) + Cantor) + Cantor)
  : I0 (ff_flip x) = f_flip (I0 x)
    := ap10_equiv
         (Inat (f (point (BAut Cantor))) (f (point (BAut Cantor)))
               (equiv_sum_symm Cantor Cantor))
         x.
(** The value of this is that we can detect which summand an element is in depending on whether or not it is fixed by [f_flip] or [ff_flip]. *)

Definition f_flip_fixed_iff_inr (x : Cantor + Cantor + Cantor)
  : (f_flip x = x) <-> is_inr x.
split; intros p; destruct x as [[c|c]|c]; simpl in p.

Definition ff_flip_fixed_iff_inr
        (x : Cantor + Cantor + Cantor + Cantor)
  : (ff_flip x = x) <-> (is_inr x + is_inl_and is_inr x).

goals.conclusion: ff_flip x = x <-> is_inr x + is_inl_and is_inr x
ff_flip x = x <-> is_inr x + is_inl_and is_inr x

contents: split; intros p; destruct x as [[[c|c]|c]|c]; simpl in p.
destruct p as [e|_]; [ elim e | reflexivity ].
destruct p as [_|e]; [ reflexivity | elim e ].
(** And the naturality guarantees that [I0] preserves fixed points. *)

Definition I0_fixed_iff_fixed
        (x : Cantor + Cantor + Cantor + Cantor)
  : (ff_flip x = x) <-> (f_flip (I0 x) = I0 x).

goals.conclusion: ff_flip x = x <-> f_flip (I0 x) = I0 x
ff_flip x = x <-> f_flip (I0 x) = I0 x

contents: (** Putting it all together, here is the proof of our claim about [I0]. *)

Definition I0_preserves_inr
        (x : Cantor + Cantor + Cantor + Cantor)
  : (is_inr x + is_inl_and is_inr x) <-> is_inr (I0 x).

goals.conclusion: is_inr x + is_inl_and is_inr x <-> is_inr (I0 x)
is_inr x + is_inl_and is_inr x <-> is_inr (I0 x)
is_inr x + is_inl_and is_inr x <-> f_flip (I0 x) = I0 x
is_inr x + is_inl_and is_inr x <-> ff_flip x = x

contents: (** Now we bring quasi-idempotence into play. *)

Definition J (Z : BAut Cantor)
  : I Z +E 1
    = I (f Z).

goals.conclusion:
equiv_path (Z + Cantor + Cantor) (Z + Cantor) (Ip Z) ..1 +E 1 =
equiv_path (Z + Cantor + Cantor + Cantor) (Z + Cantor + Cantor)
  (Ip (f Z)) ..1

equiv_path (f (f (f Z))).1 (f (f Z)).1 (ap f (Ip Z)) ..1 =
equiv_path (Z + Cantor + Cantor + Cantor) (Z + Cantor + Cantor)
  (Ip (f Z)) ..1

contents: (** We reach a contradiction by showing that the two maps which [J] claims are equal send elements of the third summand of the domain into different summands of the codomain. *)

pose (x := inl (inr (fun n => true))
               : ((f (point (BAut Cantor))) + Cantor) + Cantor).

hypotheses.body: inl (inr (fun _ : nat => true)) : f (point (BAut Cantor)) + Cantor + Cantor
inl (inr (fun _ : nat => true)) : f (point (BAut Cantor)) + Cantor + Cantor
inl (inr (fun _ : nat => true)) : f (point (BAut Cantor)) + Cantor + Cantor
inl (inr (fun _ : nat => true)) : f (point (BAut Cantor)) + Cantor + Cantor
inl (inr (fun _ : nat => true)) : f (point (BAut Cantor)) + Cantor + Cantor

contents: (** Let's make the important conclusions available globally. *)
